Hey, all right, welcome back. Okay, hey, that was a pretty easy start, right? So now what we're gonna do is we're gonna go into each of these business categories in each of their sales processes in detail. And I'm gonna give you the roadmap, like literally a process map, for each one of these processes to show you how to make them better. And I'm gonna give you a checklist of all the cool things that you could do. So when you're either looking at a client's business or when you're looking at your own and you're like, how can I make this thing more profitable using all of the stuff I already have, without having to go get more traffic or whatever, you'll be able to just pull up your roadmap, pull up the blueprint and whip out the old checklist and be like, okay, that's exactly what I'll do. So we're gonna start with a process that's super familiar to everybody, which is the information marketing business category and specifically the loss leader sales process. So loss leader sales process is like a book funnel or even my four courses for $4 thing. That's a loss leader sales process. And that's the one that's working so well right now with my ad. So I think it's probably pretty fitting that we start with a deep dive into that one. All right, so without further ado, let's get to it. And also don't forget that beneath this video or somewhere near this in your members area here, there is a download to the checklist and the process maps for you. Same thing that I'm gonna be referencing in the videos that we're about to see in three, two, magic of editing, one, let's go. All types of different businesses out there. So we're literally just starting with this one because I think it's likely to have a high degree of familiarity to you. If you don't sell information, you've certainly bought plenty and have witnessed the sale of lots of information. So that's why we're starting with this. As well as the fact that information marketing is as old as the hills. And what's awesome is the stuff that worked a hundred years ago still works real good. And there's kind of like a handful of standard sales processes and business models. And that's kind of it. And once you sort of know the templates, you kind of got it down, which is awesome. So let's get jigging with here. So process number one, model, however you wanna define it, would be the old event-based ascension. All right, now this one is like really as old as the hill. This goes all the way back to revivalist church tent stuff. This is super, super old and established. This is a brilliant, very, very effective, very profitable model and process for selling information. And so it looks like this. You got yourself a free or cheap front end event. People still do this this very day, by the way. Instead of being held in tents though, now it's held in little hotel ballrooms. And so that's usually typically free, typically known as a preview event. And if the people are doing a good job and they're ethical and good people, they give genuinely valuable information there and actually help people. And they use the oldest trick in the book, which is to help them by, demonstrate you can help them by actually helping them and then they upsell to the next event. So that next event might be a two day boot camp or something like that. And that event, if they're good people, they're giving really, really, really good information and over promising and not over promising, rather over delivering on their promises. And then they'll make an offer to like the big thing. So they're really in the big thing business. The big thing could be a mastermind, it could be coaching, it could be consulting, it could be franchising, it could be anything. But the way they're selling it is through this particular process and model. And so that's how the event based ascension thing for selling information works. And then you've got the continuity based ascension. And this is like kind of the model more than the process really. So continuity based ascension, as you start with a loss leader, it doesn't have to be, but usually you get a member of a continuity program and generally speaking, you're spending more than you're getting in your first month to get them. So you might acquire a full fledged paying member to say your thing is $100 a month or your client's thing is $100 a month. You might cost you 200 bucks to get that $100 a month person. Or you might sell a free trial and that free trial might cost you $30 to get a free trial person or whatever. And then that person is taking a trial to become a member and then they ascend from, I'm taking this straight out of Dan Kennedy who I've studied for years and have been a client and customer of forever. You know, you got your multiple levels, right? So regular and then there's gold level and then there's the platinum and the diamond and the diamond encrusted flying platinum unicorn level and all of that kind of stuff. And that has been around forever and ever and ever. And of course with every level of ascension, they get more stuff, you know? And so none of these are better than the other, by the way, I'm just kind of giving you an overview of, here's kind of the lay of the land of how information marketing works in general. My animations are weird here. There's a whole other category that I guess will show up in the middle at the end. You've got some clients that'll have just a whole suite of products with no real, I love these clients, by the way, with like no ascension and nothing actually tying the products together. Like no cross promotion, no nothing. These are, you really want to get as many of these as you can because you're like, hey, after you sell them, the first product, why don't we build a little sequence that sells them the second product or something? And you know, I've got clients that'll do this and they'll have like all of their products at the same cost and they're all to the same market but they don't ever tie them together. So the guy buys the thing A, he never gets a pitched for thing B. It just happens to have bought thing A and that's it. And this is very, very common, by the way. So I just call that the product suite where he's got all kinds of people selling stuff but with no real organization to it. Then there's the one main thing model. And this is generally where someone has like one specific offer and that's it, the end. Real good example of this are the fellas from clientsandfunnels.com, no I'm sorry, trafficandfunnels.com. Like they pretty much just sell one thing which is a coaching program on how to get clients and stuff. And like that's it. I think you can pay more for more coaching or something but that's like kind of the one thing. Jeff Walker, you know, he's actually a sentient but his primary main thing, product watch formula. What does he buy? My man's on like POF 9.8 at this point, you know? I think he's been selling it for 15 years and as he should, it's excellent. And then he's got his platinum club and his mastermind but you know, most of it's the main thing. I think the platinum level mastermind thingy or whatever was like an afterthought. It was like, well, I got all these customers, I might as well sell them something else. It's kind of rude not to. Marie Forle is another great example of this. Unless she has changed her model, she's had one thing forever called B-School and that was it. So some folks just do a thing. Great, cool, no worries, right? Then you've got product based ascension. There's our middle one right there. And this is where like in our world is like a book funnel type of things. You got a loss leader, you know? And then if they're doing it right, there's an order bump and then there's some mid tier upsells that we one click upsells. And all of this stuff right here, the purpose of all of this part is generally to get a customer that break even or better so you can try to sell them the main thing, right? And so within all of these models, right? There are all kinds of processes. So I really should have said information marketing models here because there's different sales processes for all of these. Now that doesn't mean there's like a million sales processes. There's only a few. As a matter of fact, and I want you to forgive my editing here. I was like, gonna try to build all of this into a keynote presentation for you, but I was like, you know what? It'll be more beneficial for everybody if I build these process maps and save them as PDFs for you. So let's take a look for example like the most common one, right? Which would be an information marketing business where they've got a product based ascension. And that's the last one that I've described, right? So it looks like this or something, some semblance of this traffic to a free report to a loss leader. You might be more familiar with the term tripwire, right? Then they go from there to an order form and the order form may or may not have order bumps. In fact, we'll talk about things, levers in this here in a minute, right? So traffic from an email list or whatever, opt in for the free report right after they opt in. The page says, hey, your free reports been emailed to you but buy this thing, right? They click to the order form, they buy it or they don't. And if they do buy it, they see the one click up sells, right? And then if they buy those one click up sells, now they're in the backend sequence to buy the main thing. So this person here and these products here, the real meaning of these, the real purpose of these is to acquire a customer for your client. So every dime of the main thing is profit. So their true customer acquisition cost is nothing. And in a dream situation, they're actually selling this at a profit because of these upsells, right? Like that's like the dream situation. So that's kind of like that process. You've seen it a million times. You've seen a book funnel, be seeing in my book funnels, that's it, right? And some folks in traffic straight to the sales page, which is what I do. Some folks in traffic to an opt in page, which is what I should probably consider doing. You know, and then now let's talk about like, how to make this process better, you know, just as kind of us talking. And don't worry, I'm gonna give you checklists and everything, but as you're looking at this, and let's say you got a client, right? And your client says, okay, here's my sales process. And let's say the opt in rate is 50% and the conversion rate here, I'm like, you know, everybody 50% opt in rates are real good, by the way. Let's say, you know, this thing's got a freaking 25% conversion rate, right? You know, so the copy's working great and all that kind of stuff. What would we be looking for right here? What do you think? Well, or my client, I'd be like, okay, what happens if they don't buy the loss leader, right? That'd be question number one. Question number two would be, you got an order bump? Question number three would be, is it a one step or a two step order form? All right, and then I'd start looking for those numbers. Now, we're gonna, I'm gonna give you a checklist for all of this kind of stuff. But like, if we were to, let's see, I can't draw on the screen here. If we were to draw, can I guys? I don't think I can. I was gonna try to get fancy and wait, maybe. I don't think so. Can I draw? Maybe, all right, so if we were to draw a line, right here, and just say, okay, let's only talk about this stuff on the left side of this line, we've already got some areas, by the way, some levers, right, lever number one is, assuming they're collecting opt-ins is what's happening after they opt-in. If the person doesn't buy, what are you doing, right? Lever number two is, are there order bumps on this page? If they got one, what would happen if we had two? If they ain't got one, let's put one on there, right? So those are two obvious levers right there that we could tweak in this sales process that would be game changers. Shockingly, tons of people do this deal right here, and they'll have like a three-day email sequence, and if someone doesn't come and buy the loss leader and go now, kind of go behind this line here, after three or five days or whatever, they'll put them in a quote unquote, nurture sequence where they just get an email newsletter every Friday. Like that's so common. So just fixing that one thing is huge, right? That would fall into the more follow-up category. I'm telling you, at the end of the day, after we go through nine million of these things, you're gonna be like, oh my God, Frank was totally right. 90% of the time is more follow-up or make more offers. Many times, there's no order bump here, right? So those are two huge levers. Now, let's look at the behind the scenes, right? So let's say we're dealing with everything after they buy. All right, okay. So what if they, first of all, do they have upsells? But let's assume they do. Do they have an exit pop on the upsell page? So if you put an exit intent pop-up on a one-click upsell page, it says, wait, let me get straight to the point. You can have this thing right now for this price. If you click here, you'll get an increase in conversions. Do they have one of those? That's one lever. If they don't buy these upsells, do they have an email sequence going back to the one-click upsells? 99% of clients will not. They'll just feel like, well, they didn't take the upsells, okay then. So those are two levers we can pull. If they only have one, we could revolutionize the business by adding a second upsell. So actually now we got three levers. The pop-up, multiple upsells, and emailing the list immediately right back to the upsells. So in Infusionsoft, for example, or in ClickFunnels, or in Kartra, all three of these solutions have the ability to drive a recent purchaser of this thing right back to a one-click upsell page, and the customer can still make that purchase via a one-click upsell in the same session. I mean, imagine what would happen if someone buys, let's say, the book, they see the upsell, they don't buy it, and now they're put into a 12-hour deadline sequence driving them back to that upsell. Do you think that would make a little bit of a difference there in the average order value? Absolutely, right? And then some people ain't even got a back-end sequence. Okay, so there's your process map, for how a loss-leader InfoFunnel typically works. And that process map is likely nothing shocking or nothing surprising to you. However, you might have noticed some gaps as I'm going over what most people do, where you're like, man, maybe if I did some of that stuff, I may make more money, which is ultimately what we want to have happen. And as a way of ensuring that that happens, I've actually created a checklist for you where you can analyze your business, if this is the type of sales process you're using, and you can look at every little piece of the sales process and see how it's performing, and find ways to maximize its profitability. So that checklist is, of course, in this module, and I'm gonna walk you through it right now, right? Let's go. And it starts out looking like this, beautiful blank piece of paper. So the first thing we get from the client is the big picture numbers, right? And most clients are gonna know this, for example, hey, about how many monthly visitors do you get? You know, and then how much do you spend on ads? All right, so we're gonna figure that out, and what's the monthly gross revenue for the whole business, you know? And what's the monthly net revenue after ad spend? And granted, when we use the word net here, we're kinda throwing it around loosely. This isn't including office space, or salaries, or any of that kind of stuff. We're just talking specifically about net after ad spend, that type of net figure. And I've never had a client get all super granular and weird about that. So you probably won't either, but that's what I mean. When I say monthly net revenue, net over ad spend. And so this kind of stuff is usually pretty easy to get, right? We also wanna know how many new customers do you get every month? How many new opt-ins or leads do you get every month? Same thing, by the way, opt-in lead, whatever. I'm gonna use the term interchangeably. Then, sometimes they won't know this, what's your average customer value? And this is one where it could get a little bit harder for them to figure out. So they're like, well, man, my phone holds like six months long or whatever, then just try to find the average customer value over a 30-day period. But for this specific process, it should be really easy to find out. So what's the average customer worth to you? And then how much does it cost to acquire one? And by the way, the way you figure that out is you take monthly new customers here, it will actually take your monthly ad spend and you divide it by monthly new customers and that's how much it costs you to get a customer, right? And then we look to find, okay, well, what's your net profit per customer, right? So those are the big picture numbers that you would get kind of regardless of what the business was. Maybe if you're dealing with a retail establishment, we might not necessarily have leads or opt-ins or anything. If your client happens to be a clothing store or you're dealing with walk-in traffic or whatever. But generally speaking, these are gonna be all the big picture metrics that you would get. So now we start to kind of dig in, right? So we know, because you've already been working with this client, we know what kind of business they have and we know it's an info business and we know they sell books and stuff or whatever. So now what we wanna do is we just wanna have a checklist and see if they've got all the critical components that they should have for their sales process, right? So we're basically just gonna go through this and be like, okay, do you have an opt-in page before your offer, all right? So some loss leader funnels don't, like my book funnels don't. And as I've built this for you, I'm like, dude, I should totally have an opt-in page before my offer. But I don't, right? So it's just yes or no, right? So you would just put a little check box here, yes. And if they don't, then you would leave it blank, right? And then you wanna look and see, okay, do you have a two-step order form? Two-step order form is simply, I'm sure you already know what this means, but it means they enter their name, email address and phone number and stuff, and then they hit next and then they go to the part where they pay, right? Now, the reason that's smart to do is because so many people abandon the cart and they don't finish their order, you could follow up with them. So, you know, we have a little check mark there, do they or don't they, whatever. Do they have an order bump, right? So if they're selling a little $5 book or $10 book or whatever, is there a bump where it's, hey, get this other thing for 40 bucks or whatever, you know, do they have one of those? Do they have a one-time offer, right? AKA a one-click upsell. Do they have a one-click downsell? Do they have another one-time offer, right? Ideally, I like to see three, incidentally, three one-click upsells in any loss leader funnel. Personally, right? And so this incidentally, the second one doesn't have to be a downsell, it could literally be like just three, one-time, you know, one-click upsell offers, three different products, doesn't matter. But, you know, check accordingly, right? You kind of get it. Do they have an exit intent pop on their OTOs? So an exit intent pop on an OTO means the client bought, you know, the customer rather, they bought, let's say, the book and now they're watching the video pitch for the one-click upsell and they go to leave, that's when a little pop-up comes and goes, all right, all right, all right, I know you don't want to watch the video, but here's the pitch. And then it kind of tries to save the sale, you know. So do they have one of those? Yes or no? Real simple, right? Is there a deadline sequence to non-buyers of the main offer? Meaning, if they opt in for something before they see the sales page for the main thing, in this case, the little loss leader, you know, the book or tripwire or whatever you want to call it, right? What happens is there a deadline sequence if the people don't buy, right? Yes or no? Real simple, okay? And then is there a deadline sequence to people who don't take the order bup, assuming there is an order bup? And is there a deadline sequence to people who don't buy the OTOs, right? So like, we got three of them here, unless somebody buys all three, makes a little bit of sense to kind of determine which one they didn't buy and send them a series of emails with a little timer to it, back to the one they didn't buy, you know? So do they have that? Yay or nay? Do they retarget the people who land on their page and don't buy? With like retargeting ads? Yay or nay? Do they have a deadline sequence to cart abandoners, assuming they've got a two-step order form? Yay or nay, you know? So all we're doing is just like clicking, you know, we're like doing a check mark or whatever. That's it, we're not trying to tell them what to do here. We're literally just like, okay, you got this, you got this, you got this, yes, no, yes, no, okay, cool. Right, so really, really simple part of the process here. And now what we wanna do is, by the way, these are kind of all of the pieces of this, whoops, hang on, this pie, right? So all those components I just listed, those are all the stuff that makes this thing work, excluding that part, right? So that's all that checklist was, was like, hey, do you have all the stuff that could maximize this sales process right here? So, no, I gotta go through all the animations, sorry. So that's all that was, that's all we were doing, right? Just seeing if they got the stuff. So now what we wanna do is we wanna get the numbers behind each component of the process. So if they have an opt-in page, we would wanna know what the opt-in page conversion rate is, right, and so you would put it in this little box here. Or do this however you want, like you don't have to print these out, if you don't want to, you know, I do all mine on a notebook, I just made this for you, because it looks cool. I just do it on a little notepad, you know, because I've been doing it so long, I kinda got it by memory, right? And now we wanna know, okay, what's your main sales page conversion rate, right? So like, I know mine, my book funnel page, typically around 5% from cold traffic, generally speaking. All right, what's your order form step one conversion rate? Now this is assuming they have a two-step order form, right? So like if they don't have any of this stuff, like if they don't have an opt-in page before their main sales page, we wouldn't mess with it. And nor would we begin trying to talk them into one. Like all we're doing right now is just getting data. So if they don't have a two-step order form, then this would be irrelevant, we wouldn't put anything here, you know? But if they do have a two-step order form, we're gonna put the conversion rate here. How many people opt-in to step one of your order form? And then what's your order form conversion rate? This is a big deal, by the way. I don't wanna jump ahead, but this could be a major lever that like really, really transforms a business, just getting order form conversions up. But that's a story for another day. All right, let's go further. So next thing we wanna do is if they have an order bump, what's the order bump conversion rate, right? So just getting data, that's it. And a lot of times they won't know it off the top of their head, but like every freakings, you know, thing out there can keep up with this. Entrepreneur would be able to pull a report, you know, quick funnels easy, like quick funnels totally pulls reports on these, like no problem, infusion softs, like all of this data is gettable. And never forget that the clients you're dealing with, they're the sweet spot clients, right? Between half a million and five million bucks. So they didn't get there by being dumb and by not doing anything. So chances are they know this stuff or they can go figure it out without it being a big deal. I've never had a client be like, I have no idea, everything's horrible. You know, no, I have had clients like not know deeper funnel numbers and that's fine. You just like help them find them onward. So then assuming they've got a one click up sell, what's the conversion rate on that sum, bitch? And then assuming they have a second one, which I called a down sell, but whatever, let's just assume, like, you know, you have three opportunities for someone to buy something via one click after the transaction. So what's the conversion rate on the second one, whether it's a down sell or just a second product and same for the third one. What's the conversion rate here? Right. And then I did this on purpose because I had actually when making the slide for you, duplicated OTO3 conversion rate. And then as I was animating the slide, I was like, oh, how do I fix that? So I just deleted it. That's why there's that space there. Now I wanna know how many emails are in their follow ups to the opt-ins assuming they have that, right? So assuming they've got an opt-in page before they see the trip wire thing, how many emails are in your follow ups, right? And next thing we wanna know, how many emails are in the follow up to the card abandons, assuming they've got a two step order form in the first place, right? How many emails are in the follow up sequence for the order bump, right? So if someone buys the stuff but they don't buy the order bump, you know, do you follow up with them? If you don't, you should. And if you do, how many emails you got? By the way, I don't do a lot of this stuff personally. So as I'm building this for you, I'm like, oh my God, I'm so stupid. So it's very, very rare. You're gonna find a business that's actually doing all of this stuff. So it's real hard not to help people once you start really getting into the Sandor methodology. How many emails are in the follow up for the first one, click up sell? How many emails are in the follow up for the second one? And how many emails are in the follow up for the third one? Right? And so that's kind of the big picture numbers here for the process components. Like this is what we need to find out, right? And then sometimes you can get this data and well, you can always get this data and sometimes it's helpful and sometimes there's nothing to it. But since you're getting data, you might as well find out just in case there's something funky and that's the numbers for the email sequence. And you can get as granular as you want, right? Like what's the open rate for email number one? What's the click through rate for email number one? I generally don't do that. I just find out, okay, we got our main follow up, right? That would be assuming there's an opt in page before the main sales letter. So there might not be by the way, but assuming there is, we got open rate and click through rate. Just wanna get the general blend for each one. Every email system ever can tell you, by the way, like, you know, for the past 30 days with the open rate and click through rates generally for the entire campaign or so it's not like it's hard to find out. And then we wanna get it for the rest of the components. Right? So, okay, card abandonment, open rate and click through rate if they have a card abandonment sequence. All right? And then bump abandon, open rate, click through rate. And then I did it for three OTOs. And all we're doing is just saying, okay, you know, do you have the data? And what we're looking for in this is like if there's something really off, you know, so like if the main follow up open rate is 1% and the click through rate is like 0.003, then we know something's tore up, you know? So that's all you're doing. You're just like a doctor going, okay, well, have you been feeling dizzy? You know, do you have any odd bleeding coming out of your ears or anything like that? Basically same thing. All right? And so our last little box here is G-O-O. And this is where you would write something down. G-O-O stands for glaringly obvious observations. All right? So like if you were doing this with me, okay, and I was your client and we were going through the frankkernbook.com book funnel which is a loss leader funnel, like classic funnel, right? Then here's what we would discover about your old boy Frankie, okay? First of all, there's no opt in page before the offer. Don't know if that's a big game changer yet. I do not have a two step order form, dumb. Okay. This is therefore I don't have a deadline sequence to card abandoners. I do not have a deadline sequence to people who don't take the order bump. Order bumps an extra $27 or $37. I forgot the price. But whatever the hell it is, I ain't got one, right? I have a deadline sequence to, I don't even actually have a deadline sequence. I have one email that goes out for the very first one time offer. And I actually don't do retargeting ads to non-buyers, right? So like glaringly obvious observation, if I were your client would be, okay, what's like the one thing we could do immediately that would get more money? And it would probably be, let's do this deadline sequence to non-buyers of OTOs. And you might be thinking, okay, Frankie, why did you come up with that? And the logic is that, well, my order bump is $37 and my one time offers there, the minimum one is $194, right? So it makes a lot more sense to build a deadline sequence to the stuff that's got the higher ticket price, right? And these one time offers are already built. So it's not like I'd have to go and build any more web pages or something. I just got to build an email sequence. So I would write like glaringly obvious observation right there, like get current to put deadline sequence to non-buyers of OTOs in place, right? And then there would be a tool for that. And so part of your job, by the way, is to not be like, okay, dude, you need to test putting an opt-in page before you're offered, test a two-step order form. And then what we need to do is we wanna build a deadline sequence to everyone who doesn't buy the book. And then we need to build a deadline sequence to everybody who doesn't buy from the two-step order form. And then we need to make sure you got retargeting ads on. And then we gotta build a deadline sequence to non-buyers of the order bump. And then we gotta build a deadline sequence to every single one of your OTOs. Like that's gonna make your client flip out. They're gonna be like, oh, God, that's too much. So what you do is you look at all of this stuff. And if anything's glaringly obvious, like damn, dude, really, there's no deadline sequence to people who don't buy your $300 one-time offer. Why don't we put that in to start with? So that's kind of what that is. And so remember earlier in the very beginning of this week, I was like, okay, I'm gonna kind of be showing you the numbers and the diagnosis and the one thing at the same time. That's kind of what I meant. Like when you do this, it becomes really freaking obvious. Or I like, oh, man, okay, let's put one of these things in. And then when we get this data, incidentally, we could be like, all right, well, let's see here. All everything's looking good. Like anything under 20% on an opt-in page conversion needs to be fixed, by the way. Sales page conversion, let's say, they're driving a ton of traffic, right? And there's no opt-in and their sales page is converting like low, okay, on a $5 offer or whatever. And we look at it and we're like, huh, this is weird. And then because you can't see anything glaringly wrong with the sales copy, it's not like, it's like, hey, screw you. Or whatever. And then we go and we learn that the order form conversion rate is like 2%. You should always have a minimum 10% on an order form, right? Well, that would be a glaringly obvious observation. You'd be like, oh, dude, just the order form. Order form is broken. So we wouldn't go rewrite the entire sales letter. In this case, we'd try to fix the order form. We'd be like, hell, if we can get the order form from 2% to 10%, we 5x the freaking business, right? So that's where these kind of come into play. You're looking for things that are like really, really off. So if number of emails and follow-ups to opt-ins, let's assume that the funnel's like the classic funnel, where it's opt-in to sales letter, for the little trip wire thingy, and then the order bumps and upsells and all that kind of stuff. If the number of emails and follow-up to opt-ins is like three, and it's one every other day for a week, and then they go into the weekly newsletter on Friday, well, that's a glaringly obvious thing, right? You're like, okay, here's what we could do as we can put like, you know, a deadline funnel right there on the front, and we're gonna send them seven emails over the course of two days, or even 12 hours to get them to buy that sucker before the price goes up. So we'll get deeper into this now, but what I want, not now, but we'll get deeper into all of this stuff later, what I wanna do for you in the beginning parts of our work together is kind of give you these big picture overviews for the main sales processes for every business category, and have you practice doing this and see where you can find some glaringly obvious stuff, because I can tell you, you know, 20 years in the game, right, very rare, and let's assume we're leaving this part out, very rare in this particular, and now granted, I'm not assuming every single client you're having is going to have a info business with a product-based ascension model using a loss leader. I mean, just literally, we're just starting with this particular sales process because you've seen it before and you're familiar with it. It's extremely rare, as in I've literally never seen it happen in my life, that anybody's got all of this stuff dialed in, right? So as you're going through this stuff here, you're going to get really, really good at finding the one thing, like just as you're doing this part, you know? So as you're going through this checklist and as you're going through these numbers, generally speaking, the diagnosis and discovery of the one big thing to do, and the multiple things to do, but the discovery of what to do in what order happens really, really quickly. With my private client group, who we work with for a year, you know, so it's a year-long engagement, I joke around with them and I'll do this with them. I don't actually have a checklist or whatever because I've done it for so long, although now that I've built this, I'm like, now I'm going to start using this thing for myself because this is pretty good. I miss some of my own stuff here. But I will joke with the client because our meetings are two days, you know? And it's usually like a little group of eight or 10 clients or whatever. And I run Sandor on every single client in the meetings, you know, it's like, what's your objective and all that kind of stuff. And I'll tease them and I'll be like, okay, well, I'm just gonna spend most of the rest of the day trying to figure out how to make it look like it took me all the way until tomorrow after lunch to figure out what to tell you to do. You know, because it becomes so glaringly obvious what to do. Then the client doesn't mind, by the way, your client is not gonna be like, oh, I wish it was something that was complicated and difficult. No, they want something glaringly obvious to fix. Right?
